content:
- Ramki Gaddipati Office Hours – Structured Meeting Summary

- II. Overall Context of the Conversation/Call

- This Office Hours call focused on a broad set of technical and operational areas within Zeta's platform infrastructure and product functions, prioritizing architectural decisions, operations visibility, security enforcement, and process robustness. Ramki provided directional insight on how unauthenticated API traffic should be handled—emphasizing enforcement at the network edge—and critically challenged existing approaches to configuration, policy management, and file/batch processing. Several flows (including payments, provisioning, and reconciliation processes) were debated, with a clear push from Ramki for data integrity, declarative configuration, operational autonomy, and systemic reduction of manual dependencies. The group also tackled practical matters of incident response, retry logic, intermediary storage, reporting/data presentation, and the need for the operations team to stay in control with technical flexibility. Ramki anchored the meeting with detailed reviews, clarifications on architectural calls, and assignments for team follow-up, keeping each track on actionable next steps.

- IV. Key Takeaways from the Call

- Enforcement Must Move To Edge: Ramki emphasized all unauthenticated/suspicious traffic should be filtered at the earliest ingress point, not allowed to traverse internal clusters ("I don't want any unauthenticated request to reach any application"; "reject at the leftmost point").
- Policy Injection Approach Critiqued: Teams must develop a mechanism for hierarchical policy configuration and injection to plugins, ensuring declared routing logic, tenant policy, and operational constraints are enforced and easily managed (discussion of Kong/Cloudflare/OPA).
- Declarative Configurations Only: All configuration (including traffic policies and resource declaration) should be strictly handled via Terraform or similar declarative methods, ensuring no manual/imperative drift.
- Malicious Traffic Handling: Focus not on origin but on how soon and reliably it is blocked; explicit guidance was given to restrict unauthenticated endpoints and reduce allowed public endpoints to as few as possible.
- Batch Processing and Reconciliation: Application and batch inputs/outputs must have robust envelope/metadata for visibility and audit; operational workbench should allow tracking, status, and aging, with a push for a “flattened view” for support functions.
- Retry and Failure Handling Philosophy: Persistent automation and retry mechanisms should be lightweight and give ultimate control to operations for manual intervention, instead of deeply embedding complex retry flows.
- Data Pipeline Optimization: Redshift should not be used as a primary operational analytics DB for highly active workloads; guidance towards utilizing replicas with SQL editor access for production support teams.
- Push Provisioning Crypto Requirements: Confirmed that payment HSMs are unnecessary for simple encryption use-cases (e.g., push provisioning payloads); recommended using KMS-backed solutions as more appropriate.
- 5 Nines/Uptime Discussions Deferred: Key availability architecture discussions for Cypher and related services scheduled for a focused deep-dive in the next session with essential stakeholders.
- Configurable Payment Reversal Handling: Introduced flexibility to delegate late reversals to clearing modules, flag exceptional cases, and keep operations informed.

- V. Ramki's Insights

1. Edge-First Security Posture: Ramki firmly stated that all policy enforcement (authentication, whitelisting, traffic filtering) must be done as close to the network edge as possible—preferably before any request is allowed deeper into the system. He pressed the need to avoid unnecessary load and exposure further in the stack, expressing both technical rigor and an operational mindset for minimizing blast radius.

2. Unified, Programmable Policy Injection: He called for a programmable, hierarchical approach to policy distribution that policy information should be available at cluster, zone, and edge levels, accessible by all plugins/services. He critiqued ad-hoc, distributed approaches and pressed for a system-managed, injected policy framework, with a declarative mechanism through resources like Terraform, ensuring clarity and reproducibility.

3. Operational Control and Observability: Ramki consistently advocated for creating transparent, observable data flows—especially for batch and file-based processes. He insisted on checkpoints, batch-level metadata, grouping (envelope concept), and coherent audit trails, so operations can reconcile, retry, or intervene when necessary. This included building flexible tools—such as a SQL workbench on a read replica—for support teams to get full situational visibility with minimal engineering friction.

4. Automation Philosophy – Manual Override: Automation should always allow for eventual manual override. While retries and reminders can be built in flexible technical ways (ranging from lightweight cron/reminder systems to batch workflows), operations should maintain ultimate authority to intervene or forcibly progress/retry workflows as required, ensuring customer or business commitments are not blocked by technical edge-cases.

5. Critical Evaluation of Tooling and Data Stores: He cautioned against improper use of analytics tools like Redshift for operational/real-time queries and pushed to keep analytical and transactional workloads separated, encouraging leveraging read replicas for live operations reporting and SQL querying.

6. Pragmatic Treatment of Security/Cryptography: Demonstrating a practical approach to cryptography, Ramki made it clear that complex payment HSMs are not always required for every scenario. He applied technical judgment, suggesting KMS-backed solutions when dealing with non-sensitive keys or simple data encryption flows required for features like push provisioning.

- VI. Ramki's Suggested Action Items

1. Task: Collaboratively design and implement a mechanism for hierarchical policy repository/injection, enabling edge-level enforcement for route-specific configs and whitelisting.
- Assigned To: Aaditya Tripathi, Chetan Piduru (with participation from Praveen Darshanam for Cloudflare alignment)
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Work jointly; ensure configuration is pushed through the zone config, managed via Terraform, with policy made available at all ingress/egress points and support for plugin-injection.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: Come back with a concrete direction/solution (timeline not explicitly mentioned, but marked as priority).

2. Task: Audit all unauthenticated endpoints across environments, justify their existence, and provide a drastically reduced, tightly-controlled list.
- Assigned To: Sridhar Rao Errabelli, Praveen Darshanam
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Complete a full audit of endpoints, justify why each must be public/unauthed; minimize list to 1-5 endpoints if possible.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: No explicit deadline, but urgency implied.

3. Task: Adjust web application architecture so that only static assets are served on non-auth protected endpoints, segregating static and dynamic flows.
- Assigned To: Nishant Agarwal (with collaboration from Hercules team)
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Serve all static resources (JS, CSS, images) via dedicated endpoints and avoid serving them through app gateways.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: Not provided.

4. Task: Enable operational SQL access via a flattened view built on OLTP read replica for application/reconciliation status queries within application workbench.
- Assigned To: Gowthamarajan M R, Divya Das, Application Workbench Team
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Assemble a flattened (preferably materialized) view with relevant operational fields, surface it through a console with SQL editor, avoid putting load on the primary DB, and prevent ad-hoc analytics against Redshift.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: As soon as practical; suggested as a near-term deliverable.

5. Task: For Push Provisioning feature, verify cryptographic requirements and migrate to simpler KMS-backed encryption if technically sound, validating AWS Payments Cryptography Service or equivalent.
- Assigned To: Nishant Agarwal
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Share AWS Payment HSM documentation with Ramki, but move forward with KMS-based solution where feasible, avoiding unnecessary cost/complexity.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: None explicitly.

6. Task: Prepare for follow-up discussion on 5 nines Cypher availability by ensuring multiregion input includes additional stakeholders.
- Assigned To: Chetan Piduru, Shubham Jha (get Vivek Anand involved)
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Schedule next session when critical participants are available; cover multiregion deployment thoroughly.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: By next Office Hours session.

- VII. Jira Ticket

Scope:
- "Design and implement hierarchical policy repository and injection mechanism for plugin-based API policy enforcement at edge, zone, and cluster ingress/egress, managed through Terraform, with comprehensive whitelist and routing controls.”
- Assignee: Aaditya Tripathi, Chetan Piduru (joint)
- Justification: Captures Ramki’s repeated demand for an engineered, centrally managed, programmable enforcement infrastructure allowing consistent, scalable security enforcement at all ingress points. It is foundational for multiple areas (API whitelisting, quota/fair usage, future policy expansion).

- "Build and expose operational read-optimized, flattened view for application aging, reconciliation, and batch audit via SQL workbench to support team."
- Assignee: Application Workbench Team (Gowthamarajan M R, Divya Das)
- Justification: Directly implements Ramki’s repeated instruction for improved operational visibility, support self-service, and analytics separation—enhancing troubleshooting, escalations, and real-time reviews.

Other action items do not warrant Jira creation:
- The endpoint audit and static resource segregation are operational/architectural tasks rather than new features or improvements captured by Jira.
- Cryptographic implementation decision for push provisioning requires technical due diligence and product input before converting to a software project.
- Scheduling/follow-up actions are meeting ops, not project deliverables.

- VIII. Follow Up Questions/Suggestions

- Confirm with product/operations teams which endpoints absolutely must remain public and ensure those can be justified in security reviews.
- Verify with DevOps/Infra that the new policy config/store approach does not introduce latency or deployment bottlenecks; run performance tests in a staging environment.
- Evaluate read replica performance/capacity ahead of exposing to support or SQL console usage to avoid operational "noisy neighbor" risk.
- Plan and document the application Workbench filters/fields to ensure all operational queries (aging, status, user identifiers) are fully supported.
- Set up a regular checkpoint to revisit batch and application reconciliation accuracy after the first full production cycle on the new logic.
- Confirm AWS KMS/cryptography support for the specific encryption patterns/keys before decommissioning the HSM-based approach.
title: 2025-03-05_Office Hours (6)
